notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s how a solo 401(k) vs SEP IRA shakes out for your consulting income this year, focusing on the tradeoffs you asked about.

Big picture:

Solo 401(k): more flexible, better for maxing contributions at lower income and keeping backdoor Roth clean, but more admin and earlier setup deadline.
SEP IRA: simpler and later deadline, but usually needs higher income to hit the same contribution and can mess with backdoor Roth.
1. Contribution limits: employee vs employer

Solo 401(k)

Two contribution types:
Employee deferral: Up to $24,500 in 2026 (plus $7,500 catch‑up if you’re 50+), shared across all 401(k)/403(b) plans (day job + solo).
Employer profit‑sharing: Generally up to 20% of net self‑employment income (after half SE tax and deductions), capped by the overall $69,000 limit (under 50).
Because of the employee piece, you can hit high contribution amounts even at lower consulting income.

SEP IRA

Employer contributions only: generally 20% of net self‑employment income, up to the same $69,000 cap.
To match a solo 401(k) contribution, you usually need much higher consulting income, since there’s no employee deferral.

Key takeaway:
Modest or variable consulting income → solo 401(k) usually lets you contribute more than a SEP at the same income level.

2. Deadlines & flexibility

Solo 401(k)

Must be opened by 12/31 of the tax year to make employee deferrals for that year.
Employee deferrals must be elected by 12/31 (funding can sometimes be a bit later depending on entity type).
Employer contributions can be made up to your tax filing deadline (plus extension).

SEP IRA

Can be opened and funded up to your tax filing deadline, including extensions.
That gives you more time to see your actual profit, then decide how much to put in.

Key takeaway:

If it’s after year‑end and you didn’t set up a solo 401(k), a SEP is usually the only option for that tax year.
If you’re before 12/31, setting up a solo 401(k) keeps the employee deferral option alive.
3. Backdoor Roth IRA impact

This is where they really diverge.

The backdoor Roth IRA is affected by the pro‑rata rule, which looks at all pre‑tax traditional, SEP, and SIMPLE IRA balances on 12/31.
SEP IRA balances do count in that calculation, which can make the backdoor Roth much less tax‑efficient.
Solo 401(k): balances are not included in the pro‑rata rule.

Key takeaway:
If you’re doing or want the option to do backdoor Roth IRA contributions, people in that situation usually favor a solo 401(k) and avoid pre‑tax SEP/traditional IRA balances.

4. When each is typically better

Solo 401(k) is often better when:

You want to maximize retirement contributions from relatively modest consulting income.
You already have a W‑2 job with a 401(k) and need to coordinate:
The $24,500 employee limit is shared across all 401(k)/403(b) plans.
The employer portion is separate per business.
You care about backdoor Roth IRA and want to avoid the pro‑rata issue.
You want Roth contributions on your self‑employment income (via Roth employee deferrals).
You’re okay with a bit more paperwork and plan setup.

SEP IRA is often better when:

You want simplicity and almost no admin.
You have or might have employees and want an easy employer plan
(with a SEP you generally must contribute the same percentage for eligible employees as for yourself).
You missed the 12/31 solo 401(k) deadline and still want a tax‑deductible contribution for that year.
You’re not planning to use backdoor Roth and don’t mind pre‑tax IRA balances.
5. What matters most for your decision

Things to weigh for your situation:

Your consulting profit this year

Lower profit → solo 401(k) usually wins on how much you can put away.
Very high profit and no need for employee deferral → both can hit the same $69,000 cap.

Whether you already have a workplace 401(k)

If yes, you’ve probably used some or all of the $24,500 employee limit already.
A solo 401(k) can still help through employer contributions, but the numbers change.

Backdoor Roth now or later

If that’s on your radar, solo 401(k) is generally the cleaner fit.

Your comfort with complexity vs simplicity

Solo 401(k): more setup, and once assets hit ≥$250K, you may need Form 5500‑EZ.
SEP: very easy to open and maintain, no annual filing.

If you share:

Your approximate consulting net income for this year
Whether you have a W‑2 job with a 401(k) and how much you’re putting in there
Your age and whether you’re doing or considering a backdoor Roth

Monarch can walk through specific “with $X profit, here’s what you could put into each plan this year” scenarios.

Feature	Solo 401(k)	SEP IRA
Who can use	Self‑employed with no employees (or only spouse)	Self‑employed, can have employees
Contribution types	Employee + employer	Employer only
Employee deferral limit (2026)	Up to $24,500 (plus $7,500 catch‑up if 50+)	None
Total limit per person (2026)	Up to $69,000 (employee + employer, under 50)	Up to $69,000 (employer only, under 50)
Employer contribution formula	Generally 20% of net self‑employment income	Same 20% of net self‑employment income
Backdoor Roth impact	Does NOT interfere (no pro‑rata issue)	DOES interfere (counts in pro‑rata rule)
Roth option	Often available (Roth employee deferrals)	No Roth option
Loans	Often allowed (plan‑dependent)	No loans
Setup deadline	Must be established by 12/31 of the tax year	Can be opened and funded by tax filing deadline (plus extension)
Funding deadline	Employee by 12/31	 employer by tax filing deadline
Admin complexity	Higher (plan document, possible Form 5500‑EZ once assets ≥$250K)	Very simple, no annual filing
Best for	Maximizing contributions at modest income, backdoor Roth users, want Roth	Simplicity, later decisions, or when you have employees
